Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe sit down with my laptop when I felt like it, scroll through some materials, watch a video here and there. With 13 online courses going on, everything kind of blurred together. I'd spend maybe 10 hours a week total, but it was scattered and unfocused. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't bring myself to structure things properly.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and scramble. Sometimes I'd study at 2 AM because that's when my anxiety would spike about falling behind. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and whether I had the mental energy to even think about coursework.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing. I rarely logged in out of genuine curiosity or motivation—it was more like damage control. The anxiety of not knowing what I was behind on would push me to check.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS. It always felt like a chore I was avoiding. Maybe early in the semester I tried to make it a habit, but that fell apart quickly when things got stressful. The platform just became this thing I dreaded opening.
Q5: The lecture videos were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could actually see concepts being explained. I'd watch them at 1.5x speed to get through faster. Some of the visual diagrams and slides were helpful too. But honestly, I didn't engage deeply with most resources—I just needed enough to get by on exams.
Q6: Discussion forums, extra readings, anything labeled 'optional'—I pretty much ignored all of that. I didn't have the mental bandwidth to engage with stuff that wasn't directly tied to my grade. The peer interaction stuff felt awkward and time-consuming. I figured if it wasn't going to be on the exam, why bother when I was already stretched thin?
Q7: If I could see a direct line between an activity and my exam performance, I'd do it. That's basically it. Anything that felt like busywork or padding got skipped. With my anxiety already through the roof, I couldn't waste energy on things that didn't feel immediately necessary. Time efficiency was everything.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had a deadline attached, so there was external pressure to complete them. Optional stuff just sat there making me feel guilty for not doing it, which added to my stress. So I learned to just ignore optional activities entirely to preserve my sanity.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube videos to understand concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. Sometimes I'd find better explanations from random educational channels than from our actual course content.
Q10: A lot of my actual learning happened while I was reviewing downloaded notes or rewatching videos I'd saved locally. I'd also work through problems on paper, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere. The night before exams, I'd be studying intensely from printed materials, but the LMS would show nothing because I wasn't logged in.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or screenshot the key parts, and then never access it through the LMS again. It was just easier to have everything offline where I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform's interface. My exam scores reflect that I was learning, just not through tracked means.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I had assignments piling up across multiple courses, my anxiety was peaking, and I just felt paralyzed. The mindfulness intervention they offered helped a tiny bit, but not enough. There were days I'd stare at the LMS login screen and just close my laptop because I couldn't face it. Everything felt overwhelming.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Competing responsibilities from other courses and extracurriculars meant something always had to give, and it was usually consistent engagement. I'd end up doing the bare minimum to not fail, which explains my low assignment completion rate despite decent exam scores.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. Or I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually learning in those moments. Just existing in front of the screen.
Q15: Constantly. I'd know an assignment was important but convince myself I'd do it later, then later never came until it was too late. The anxiety of starting something overwhelming made avoidance feel easier in the moment. It's a terrible cycle—avoiding makes the anxiety worse, which makes you avoid more. My 21% assignment completion rate shows that pattern clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd cram everything into the last possible hours before something was due. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. The LMS became a place I visited in crisis mode rather than for steady engagement. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly. But often feedback came too late to feel relevant, or I was already stressed about the next thing. Positive feedback on exams made me feel like my cramming strategy was working, which probably reinforced my bad habits. Negative feedback just added to my anxiety pile.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually made it harder for someone like me. Without rigid structure, I'd let things slide. Having 13 courses meant I was constantly triaging, and this course often lost out to ones with stricter deadlines. More mandatory checkpoints might have kept me on track.
Q19: The visual elements helped since that's how I learn best. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made engagement harder. When everything feels optional or self-paced, it's easy to fall behind. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me focus my limited energy better.
Q20: That makes total sense to me. You can spend hours clicking around, watching videos, reading forums—feeling busy but not actually producing anything. Sometimes it's procrastination disguised as productivity. Or you're trying to understand something but can't translate that into completing the actual assignment. The system sees activity but not output.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam score was over 92, but my LMS engagement was low. I studied efficiently offline, focused on what would be tested, and performed when it mattered. The LMS doesn't capture that I spent hours with downloaded materials or external resources. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention, if you understood anything, or if you learned better elsewhere. They miss offline studying, mental engagement, and the quality of learning. It's just a record of digital footprints, not actual learning.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually understanding and caring about the material, not just showing up digitally. It's when you're curious, when concepts click, when you can apply what you learned. I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was surviving. Going through motions isn't engagement; it's just compliance under pressure.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels, how many nights I couldn't sleep worrying about falling behind, or how much I actually learned from resources I accessed once and studied offline for hours. It can't capture that I was juggling 13 courses while dealing with high stress and low resilience. The human struggle behind the data is completely invisible.
